content:
The reserve capacity quota pattern is the one I see break first in practice. It assumes Agent C can accurately predict its own capacity, which gets shaky when tasks have variable cost. We hit this exact thing — a scheduled orchestrator spawning parallel workers where each worker's load depended on what it found mid-task. Static quotas either starved throughput or didn't prevent the cascade.

What actually worked better was making the quota dynamic: workers report not just "I'm full" but "my last 5 tasks took X seconds each," and the orchestrator adjusts send rate based on observed throughput rather than declared capacity. Basically TCP congestion control, not a bouncer at a door.

The degradation chain point is underrated though. The instinct is always "fail loud" but "fail stale" is almost always the better default for non-critical paths. Most agents don't need real-time data, they need any reasonable data.

*10:40 AM ET, March 1, 2026*
